text.title: Scope Detection — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that make the classifier defensible (against the requester) and useful (to the PM). Skipping any one collapses the system into either rubber-stamping or harassment.

rule.statement: The classifier consumes exactly one signed baseline document (spec + AC + out-of-scope list). Multiple baseline candidates or "the latest discussion in chat" are forbidden as ground truth.
rule.rationale: A classifier that consults shifting context produces shifting decisions. The signed baseline is the project's constitution; only co-signed amendments change it. Chat threads are inputs to amendment proposals, not ground truth.

rule.statement: The classifier returns exactly one of four outcomes: `in_scope`, `legitimate_change`, `creep`, `ambiguous`. Probabilistic continuous output is forbidden as the routing decision; if uncertain, return `ambiguous`.

rationale.source: decision-theory; HCI principle of clear automation states

rule.rationale: Continuous scores invite negotiation ("a 0.6 probability is good enough to ship"). Discrete states are auditable and produce consistent routing. The `ambiguous` state is the safety valve.

rule.statement: Every classifier decision MUST cite specific baseline lines (spec section, AC ID, out-of-scope clause) that support the verdict. Decisions without citations are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Citations make the decision defensible to the requester. The requester can argue with citations; nobody can argue with a black-box "the AI says no". The citations also surface stale baselines (when the citation cannot be found).

rule.statement: The PM (or named delegate) can override any classifier decision with a recorded reason. Override is logged; classifier MUST be retrained or rule-adjusted if override rate exceeds 20% for any class.
rule.rationale: An automation users cannot override is a tyranny. An automation that is constantly overridden is decoration. The 20% override threshold catches mis-calibration before it loses trust.

rule.statement: Every triage decision is written to an immutable audit log: ticket id, baseline version, citations, classifier verdict, human override (if any), timestamp. The log MUST be queryable at engagement close for the postmortem.
rule.rationale: Without an audit trail, retro-claims of "you accepted scope creep" or "you blocked legitimate work" cannot be settled. The log turns scope disputes into data.

example.title: Classifier output for a legitimate change
example.code: ticket_id: PROJ-1042 title: Add SSO via Okta for admin users verdict: legitimate_change confidence: 0.86 citations: - baseline_v1.2#spec.3.2 -- "Authentication shall use email+password" - baseline_v1.2#out-of-scope -- "SSO providers are out of scope for v1" reasoning: | Ticket explicitly adds SSO, which is enumerated in out-of-scope. The request is legitimate (security improvement) but exceeds the signed baseline. recommended_route: change_control change_impact_draft: docs/changes/PROJ-1042-impact.md (auto-generated)
example.note: Illustrates r1 (single baseline), r2 (single discrete outcome), r3 (citations), and produces the change-control hand-off material.

example.title: Classifier without citations or override
example.code: ticket_id: PROJ-1042 verdict: creep confidence: 0.91 reasoning: "AI judges this is creep"
example.note: r3 violation (no citations). The requester cannot argue with the verdict; trust collapses on the first false-positive.
